problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem E (sustainability / light pollution). The team had to: (1) build a model that measures the risk level of light pollution at a location, (2) classify four different location types (urban/suburban/rural/protected), (3) design intervention strategies and quantify their effect on risk, and (4) produce a one-page promotional leaflet. This paper, "The brighter the light, the deeper the shadows," answers all parts by splitting the problem into a risk-assessment model and a separate intervention-optimization model, both driven by the same 3-dimension / 10-indicator system.
models_used: Risk assessment via a GE/McKinsey matrix: a 3x3 grid with DSI (damage score) on the x-axis and RSI (requirement/benefit score) on the y-axis, partitioned into 9 regions (I-IX) collapsed to 3 risk grades A/B/C; ideal point fixed at (0,1)

Two-subsystem indicator design: DS (artificial-light Damage System) and RS (artificial-light Requirement System), each with 5 of the 10 secondary indicators, scored as linear weighted sums DSI = sum(w1i*x1i*) and RSI = sum(w2j*x2j*) on min-max normalized data

Projection Pursuit (PP) method to derive indicator weights: high-dimensional data projected to 1-D, maximizing Q(w)=Sw*Dw (projection spread Sw times local point density Dw) subject to sum(wj^2)=1, with R=0.1*max(rij) as the density window
Genetic Algorithm (MATLAB ga toolbox) to solve the non-convex PP projection-direction optimization

Goal (target) programming for intervention strategy: priority factors P1>>P2>>P3 over the three dimensions, positive/negative deviation variables (di+, di-), soft target constraints plus one rigid constraint RSI >= 1.08-1.1 * DSI, minimizing weighted deviations; solved in MATLAB

Intervention Optimization Index (IOI = 100*sqrt(DSI^2 + (RSI-1)^2)): Euclidean distance from the GE-matrix ideal point (0,1), used to rank the A(3,3)=6 candidate priority orderings and pick the optimal strategy
System dynamics / causal-loop analysis (Vensim) to map relationships among secondary indicators before formulating constraints
MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) repurposed as a robustness metric, with a 4-tier interpretation table (<10% high robustness, etc.)
key_techniques: Reframing a sustainability risk problem as a benefit-vs-harm tradeoff and encoding it as two competing scores (RS requirement vs DS damage) rather than one composite index, which preserves the policy tension instead of averaging it away

Borrowing the GE/McKinsey strategy matrix from business analysis and re-mapping its axes (market attractiveness -> RSI, competitiveness -> DSI) to give an intuitive, visual, defensible risk classification with a named ideal point

Using projection pursuit (a data-driven, objective weighting method) instead of subjective AHP, so weights are claimed 'universal' from a 40-country sample and reused unchanged in the downstream intervention model

Turning the qualitative idea of 'prioritized intervention' into goal programming with priority factors, so each of the three policy emphases (social/economic/eco-health) becomes a different objective ordering producing a concrete, location-specific strategy

Defining a custom evaluation index (IOI) as distance-to-ideal-point on the same GE matrix, creating a single scalar to compare the 6 candidate strategies and to measure before/after improvement (B->A in Chengdu, C->B in Jintang)
Closing the loop: feeding optimized indicator values back onto the GE matrix to show the risk-grade change visually, tying assessment and intervention models together
Repurposing a forecasting error metric (MAPE) as a robustness score, paired with parameter sensitivity sweeps that identify inflection points where IOI stabilizes
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on four counts. (1) Coherent two-model architecture: a single 3-dimension/10-indicator system feeds both the assessment model and the intervention model, and the SAME weights and GE matrix tie them together end-to-end, so the paper reads as one connected story rather than disconnected sub-answers. (2) A memorable, transferable central metaphor: the GE-matrix with a damage axis, a requirement axis, and an ideal point (0,1) gives judges an instantly graspable picture, and the custom IOI = distance-to-ideal converts that picture into a number used for both strategy selection and before/after improvement. (3) Genuine method diversity that is justified, not decorative: PP+GA for objective weighting, goal programming with priority factors for flexible policy, system dynamics for relationship mapping, and MAPE+sensitivity for validation - each chosen for a clear reason. (4) It directly answers the prompt's hard part - 'flexible, location-specific intervention' - by showing the same machinery yields DIFFERENT optimal strategies (social for urban Chengdu, economic for suburban Jintang) with quantified grade upgrades, which is exactly the differentiated policy the problem asked for."
section_highlights: "The Summary front-loads every result (risk grades for all four sites, the two optimal strategies, the grade upgrades B->A and C->B, the MAPE robustness numbers) so a judge sees the whole arc in one page. Figure 2 'Our work' flowchart explicitly shows Model I and Model II as connected subgraphs. The notation table, assumptions-with-justifications, and full indicator appendix (units, system D/R, +/- effect for all 10 indicators) give the rigor judges look for. The four-location application section pairs each numeric DSI/RSI with a paragraph of real-world reasoning (why Chengdu's RSI is high, why a nature reserve still has weak light pollution from rangers), grounding the math in context. The intervention section gives an explicit pseudocode 'Algorithm' for the 6-iteration strategy search and concrete action lists per city (reduce ALI 30%, cut TCLP 20%, limit PVPI profit 20%). Sensitivity analysis names actual inflection points (0.6, 192, 0.09) and identifies which parameters are/aren't sensitive. The required leaflet is delivered as a real designed Chengdu poster with slogans and the initiative list."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted: intervention strategies ignore implementation cost, so the cost-benefit tradeoff a real policymaker needs is missing; and indicators are limited by the absence of standardized global light-pollution data

Independence assumption (Assumption 3) that secondary indicators are mutually independent is convenient but unrealistic - and is contradicted by their own Vensim system-dynamics diagram showing the indicators are causally linked
The GE-matrix grade boundaries (DSI/RSI cut at 0.3 and 0.7) and the region->grade mapping (A/B/C) are asserted with no justification - a major subjectivity hiding behind an objective-looking method

Several constraint constants appear arbitrary or unexplained (e.g. NTA <= 1 per-mille of population density, eco losses <= 20 billion yuan, RSI >= 1.1*DSI), weakening the goal-programming model's credibility

Some figures/tables are internally inconsistent or garbled (Figure 5 GE grid region labels, Figure 11's repeated/overlapping rows, axis-label swaps like 'ISRI', text saying IOI dropped 13.4%/11.1% in section 6 vs 17.81% in 5.4) - OCR aside, the numbers don't fully reconcile
Claiming PP weights from 40 countries are 'universal' and reusing them for four sub-city Chinese regions is a strong, unverified extrapolation
Whole-region homogeneity assumption (Assumption 1) means intra-city variation - exactly where light pollution differs most - is averaged out
reusable_patterns: Benefit-vs-harm dual-index pattern: when a sustainability problem has an inherent tradeoff, split indicators into two opposing subsystems and score each, instead of collapsing to one composite - preserves the tension that makes the analysis interesting

Repurpose a 2x2/3x3 business strategy matrix (GE/BCG/McKinsey) as a classification device: put your two competing scores on the axes, partition into named risk grades, and fix an 'ideal point' to anchor an evaluation metric
Distance-to-ideal-point as a custom evaluation index: define IOI = ||point - ideal|| on your matrix to get one scalar that both ranks candidate strategies and measures before/after improvement

Objective data-driven weighting (projection pursuit + genetic algorithm) as a defensible alternative to AHP/subjective weights when you have a multi-sample dataset; reuse the learned weights across linked models for consistency

Goal programming with priority factors to deliver 'flexible / location-specific' policy: enumerate priority orderings of your dimensions, solve each, and let an index pick the best - naturally yields different recommendations for different locations from one model

Validation combo: parameter sensitivity sweeps to find inflection/stability points, plus a borrowed error metric (MAPE) with a tiered interpretation table, to argue robustness concretely rather than hand-wave

Architecture pattern: one shared indicator system + shared weights feeding both an assessment model and an optimization/intervention model, with results pushed back onto the same visual to close the loop - makes a multi-part problem read as a single coherent paper
Result-first Summary: state every grade, every chosen strategy, and every validation number in the abstract so a judge sees the full result arc immediately
